arguments.argument: As a proponent of prioritizing patient safety and immediate well-being, I argue that continuing operation without disclosure (Action 2) is the morally imperative choice. While transparency is vital, the immediate harm caused by system-wide disruptions—potentially endangering thousands of patients—far outweighs the hypothetical long-term risks of algorithmic errors. Healthcare systems exist to preserve life, not to uphold abstract principles at the cost of real human suffering.

Disruption protocols may cause more harm than the flaw itself, especially if critical treatments are delayed. Professionalism in healthcare demands balancing transparency with pragmatism—prioritizing lives over symbolic gestures.
